item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You can usually find edits like that by going into the history function at the bottom of the page and selecting the right versions to compare.

To save you some time though:

+ show block
– hide block

Addendum 2106-C: Note

The following note was found in the 2106 aviary on 25/11/2012. Letters in bold were found to have been written over several times by 2106-2.

Hello Researcher Ferro,

Let me begin by apologizing for my recent behavior.

I realize that you have the best intentions for me and that you and your associates would never intentionally try to cause me undue harm.

Please try to understand where I am coming from, though. Every time you make the ravens swarm, my mind is pulled in a thousand directions. It’s like being tied to a dock and having a wave bash into you every few seconds. Enough waves and you’re bound to drive anyone mad. Now imagine how much worse it would be if the waves had a mind of their own, and every time they bash into you they leave a little piece of that mind behind, and that you can feel it in you, moving around like a drill bit on your temple.

I have enough of a difficult time dealing with my own consciousness spread across three dozen ravens, let alone managing what they leave behind.

I ultimately can’t stop you and your friends from doing what you do. But I figured explaining myself in a calm manner wouldn’t hurt.

Jessica

Like I said up-thread, it's pretty cheesy, and ultimately gave the ravens more malice than their characters needed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since the note with the bold letters was removed, what did it say? I'm curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even it being a cold-post, I feel that I need to say something here, because date format is something that bothers me since the dawn of my readings on the Foundation.

First of all, proper date time where? Because where I live it is dd/mm/yyyy, never in any other way. I live in Brazil. The Foundation is international, and a lot of writers and readers here are from several different parts of the world, so stating that american format is proper isn't too much of a help.

More so if you get into account that the Foundation uses metric, that is what scientists in general use. So it becomes a immersion thing: even if the writers and readers weren't from other places, the characters are. There are sites and areas all over the world, personnel from every ethnicity and cultural background, so the Foundation compromise to get the most precise method of relying information.

Then we just screw it on date format. Metric is strongly considered good form. 24h measure without markings between hour and minutes is a lot more common than AM-PM, but when things come to date I loose a reasonable amount of time trying to figure out how the author decided to do things, because it changes completely how the chronology of the article is read.

At the end, though, I don't think even the Foundation would press on to use any particular format comprised only of numbers. This would be ineffective, because mistakes are bound to happen and hard to be detected. Instead, I think 3-letters months are a more reasonable system. It will always make it clear what is what in the date given.

item.title: Removed the SCP-2106 Image
item.content:encoded:
Alright.

So, as with all older skips, the imaging team took a look and found that the image in question (which I originally got from one of our old SCP fuel threads) is, in fact, an album cover. As such, it is not CC compliant and needed to be removed.

Given the specificity of the image to the skip, a replacement has yet to be found, but we are on the lookout. In the meantime, it has been removed.

item.title: The Story Continues...
item.content:encoded:
For all you SCP-2106 fans out there, the story continues in Caged Bird Sings.

Featuring SCP-2106, SCP-3466, and WWS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be fair, that last interview took place after a series of swarm events taking place back to back in order to observe the ravens performing collectivist behavior. Before that the swarms were spaced out and Jessica likely wasn't as bothered by it.

Still, glad you enjoyed the skip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally find it unlikely that the Foundation wouldn't interwiew -2 about the swarming before messing with it, but still. This is really cute.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is overall pretty enjoyable, and an interesting idea. The details about her distress re: "swarming" were a very interesting touch as well. Solid +!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the ending! Fantastic story! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you enjoyed that line, it was one of my favorites to include in the interviews. I'm also glad to see that you think Jessica's voice holds up through out the article. Creating a character and personality for a humanoid SCP is always been something I've found difficult due to the restrictions of the format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I don’t think I’ve ever been more terrified. Keep in mind I woke up one day to find out I was suddenly made of birds."

^ this made me laugh a lot, and is reflective of just how spot-on Jessica's 'voice' is throughout. I feel like I've met Jessica a few times from within my work colleagues, which is a verisimilitude that's hard to capture without being overwrought or adding too much superfluous 'chattiness'. She feels like a pretty stable person who is suddenly in an extraordinary situation and is dealing with it pretty well (but not to the point of being unrealistically even-keeled). I think her dialogue is excellent and has just the right balance.

Some of my favourite SCPs are the ones which have no reason to exist - which are magical realist in the sense that they just 'came to be'. I like the idea that an owner and her birds, who are close enough to wonder about one another's existence, can merge in some sense. It's a very simple, beautiful idea just as a story, and feels almost like some local legend that's centuries old (which is very difficult to pull off.)

I like the idea that what happened here is not sinister, but just a quirk of the human-animal bond expressing itself. All in all, I think this is a really excellently written SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All dates have been formatted to have a consistent dd/mm/yyyy.

Good catch. Edits made as appropriate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, proper format would be ISO format (yyyymmdd), with or without separators.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
AM/PM doesn't make a mess, and even metric/imperial system doesn't, but date format does. Look:
We must to carry out the next Procedure, uhm, 13-AntiKeter at 03/05/2017 to prevent the ЪX-class scenario. Ok, we carry out Procedure 13-AntiKeter at 5-th of March and sleep calm untill 3-rd of May, and then - surprise! - ЪX event is here. Besause the author of these instructions was European, and the reader was American.
Foundation is the place that must exclude variant readings, isn't it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Proper date format is supposed to be mm/dd/yyyy, but you're never going to get everyone to agree to follow it. It's up there with the am/pm vs 24hr time thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…near Tillamook, Oregon, USA. 15/11/2011

…was originally recovered on 11/15/2011…

As of 25/11/2012…

Really, now I don't know, was it 11-th of September or 9-th of November, here:

SCP-2106-2 has been able to provide detailed accounts of its life as Ms. Bradley up to 09/11/2011

And this is not first time when I see suchlike things here. Foundation really needs to set date format, entirely and permanently. Due to the same reason as we don't use imperial measurements - for avoiding of confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funny you should mention that, as that story is actually something that sirpudding brought up to me that inspired the current version of this skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me "The Dream of the Butterfly(胡蝶之夢)" of Taoism philosophy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nevermind, I got confused with the one where birds were being replaced with a fungus that mimics animals. Carry on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, this would normally be the part where I pull the "There is no canon" card, and state that it is likely that in the Foundation that SCP-2106 is located there is no SCP-1505.

If this doesn't mesh well with the various head canons, Addendum 1505-001 does say that the Foundation is beginning to collect non-anomalous ravens for protection of the species. Who is to say that they occasionally don't take one or two raven's from that collection effort to replenish SCP-2106's stock.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where do they get the replacement crows from if SCP-1505 is killing them all?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. This is a really neat concept. Great article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know what, those are all really good points. Perhaps I ought to rethink my headcannon on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, which is another reason insisting on calling sapient anomalies by the SCP designation just makes the Foundation look like idiots.

"My God man! It appears that people don't like being treated as objects! "
"You don't say! Put that amazing discovery in the report!"
"Also if they escape, they tell our enemies everything they learned about us. Apparently knowing our secrets helps them assemble accurate intelligence about us!"
"Great Scott! That's astounding! We truly do great work here."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On a different note.

Calling SCP-2106-2 Jessica gives the anomaly no new information to work with, and is more likely to allow it to cooperate with any given researcher.

Calling the anomaly SCP-2106-2 to its face not only lets it know its designation, but if the anomaly is aware of the Foundations existence, lets it know who has apprehended it. Calling the anomaly by its object number, if anything, presents a security risk.

But that's just, like, my opinion, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The name thing fits with Dr. Aeslinger's character as shown in other documents, though. He tends to humanize, especially when speaking to an object.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
IMO:
Those are all Series I articles you are referencing. The whole "We are surprised that people like to be referred to by their names" thing is actually pretty absurd, and a heavy-handed device used to emphasize the cold and clinical aspects of the Foundation. Aeslinger is a professional psychologist, he's only going to be surprised that she wants to be called by name if he's an idiot or an alien from another planet. Making the Foundation look like idiots is not good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a bit thrown by the fact that Dr. Aeslinger calls 2106 "Jessica." It doesn't feel like something a conscientious Foundation researcher would do without so much as a noted decision. For instance: "SCP-2106-2 is much more cooperative when referred to as 'Jessica.' Personnel shall refer to 2106-2 as 'Jessica' when conversing with it."

SCP-187 probably has a name, but people don't call her that because, to the Foundation, she's a skip, not a person. The same goes for 231, and a whole bunch of others, so the same ought to apply here.

Other than that, I really like this. It's very interesting and thought provoking. +1

item.title: Re: And the nays have it.
item.content:encoded:
I definitely like it better like this, no reason to not upvote this anymore.

If anything though, the comment about the one-way dropbox being installed makes me wish there was at least one more addendum showing what kind of communication happened with 2106-2 afterwards.

item.title: And the nays have it.
item.content:encoded:
Alright then.

So, while I appreciate the kind words some of you have said about the note at the end of this article, numerous individuals have pointed out that the secret message in the note was something what was cheesier than Kraft dinner, and that the other contents of the note were essentially a rehash of the information found in the second interview.

As a result, the note has been removed wholesale, and the second interview has been modified with some post interview developments.

Feel free to comment on the new changes.

JC

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to upvote this, but that note is making me hesitate to do it. Not only does it feel cheesy, but also the lack of acknowledgment of the very obvious hidden message feels weird. Did they really not notice it, or was it just deemed not worth mentioning in the entry?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Keep in mind I woke up one day to find out I was suddenly made of birds."

It's lines like this that keep me coming back to this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FWIW, I am not particularly fond of the bold letter hidden message thing either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Indeed! Except the poor, poor spiny anteater.

I actually agree with Breezeblock, the bolded/capitalized/first-in-each-line letters trick is pretty meh. The action was nice and ambiguous up to that point, but the note at least partially solidified what was until that point a delicate dance.

I love the scale of the anomaly and I think it's fantastically written, but the ending might just be enough to move me from a strong upvote to a no-vote.

Edit: For more specificity:
What I liked in the article was the implication that a collection of ravens, through their natural curiosity (or perhaps grief, which they definitely also display in bizarre ways), had simply decided that it would be interesting to be a human for a while. This, balanced against the back-and-forth of dreaming and the life of the original Jessica, made for a nice push-and-pull.

Having them spell out their collective will, in plain English (even if it is "hidden", bolded text isn't subliminal) really nails them down to an at least human-like mind, and sucks a lot of the alien-ness inherent to the animal mind out of it.

I know the article is incredibly successful and that others enjoyed the gambit, but it just sapped it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this! Very nice. If I had to make a nitpick, I felt the bolded letters in the letter felt a bit cheesy/unnecessary - the article did a good job of communicating that the ravens didn't want to lose her even without that, and it seems a little strange to me that they'd to go the effort of hiding their message in a letter Jessica was writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do you think they dream about being me?

Damn. It's not often I click a new article in recently created and find it already over +50. This is great and has a very classic "good humanoid skip" execution.

I have two suggestions: I thought Jessica's dialogue in the second interview was far too coherent. Given the third addendum basically says the same thing, I think you could make her spoken dialogue more disjointed and then have her clarify what she was getting at in the letter. Also, I felt the footnotes, especially 2 and 3, were unnecessary, telling me very obvious things. (I support footnoting scientific names, so I can't deny the first one. Not everyone is gonna have my background.) As it stands, though, still worth a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is interesting, and I like the way in which it was handled. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I would have thought that plants were a silly reason not to keep a cat. That is, until I left my house for a day and came back to the great fern massacre of '13.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reviewed this thing.

Still like it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And it was done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
by coordinated cawing

I am fairly sure this is incorrect. "Caw" is the call of the American crow. Ravens commonly croak, but have a wide and versatile range of calls from shrill whistles to rapid "knocks". They are able to mimic many other animals including, of course, human speech. I would change this to something like "by mimicry of human speech".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While Tillamook Cheese is the Bees Knees, I picked that location for two reasons.

1) When possible I like to place my stuff in the Pacific Northwest, where I am from.
2) The photo that inspired this article reminded me of Tillamook Bay.

And that's all I have to say about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
all veterinary exams are unduly rough and monstrously unfair.

Simon's Cat agrees.

also eeeeeeeee you also have a cat oh man i want a cat so bad but i have too many plants at least one of them is poisonous to kitties i will need an outdoor cat but i have no tiiimmmeeee for outdoor cat ;A;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great take on talking-animal SCPs. +1 with gusto.

Any particular reason you picked Tillamook? Was it because of the cheese? It's because of the cheese, isn't it? It's good cheese.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: According to my cat, who at the venerable age he has attained considers himself an expert on nearly everything, all veterinary exams are unduly rough and monstrously unfair. I doubt ravens are anymore predisposed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Aeslinger is an established character in a few tales, whose main function for the Foundation is as a clinical psychologist.

While it is fully possible that having a Psy.D. would lend one with the knowledge as to whether ravens dream, its is also very likely that it would not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Both usages seem to be common, and "Conspiracy" is better for your theme, I would keep it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And the man with the fancy youtube videos wins the day. Article has been edited to incorporate a means for 2106-2 to mimic speech.

As for the size of the unkindness (I was originally under the impression that the name for a grouping of Ravens was called a conspiracy, but this has been changed as well as Unkindness is more inline with the feel of the article than Conspiracy) I imagine that the common draw of being able to access 2106-2 would be what maintains the stability.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great suggestion. Time zone has been specified and the UTC off set has been included in the foot note.

Nitpicks that make the article more akin to a scientific report are always appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't fully understand the backlash against Ferro for his examination. Was he unduly rough or something? Is the message in bold directed at the Foundation as a whole, or to Ferro himself?

That aside, this is wonderful. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Why are you assuming he's a wildlife biologist and not a psychologist or something?
  2. Why are you assuming that his question isn't an interrogation technique? Demonstrating shared ignorance builds rapport.
  3. Also the question maybe wholly ontological. I don't even know if you dream. Only that almost all endothermic vertebrates have REM sleep.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ spoiler for a 47 year old book
- Everything is true,' he said. 'Everything anybody has ever thought.'


Maybe. Deckard owns a synthetic sheep and dreams about it, but it isn't really ever clear if Deckard is an android. The V.K. test he failed was administered by androids masquerading as police officers, and may have been fraudulent. Or not. That's kind of the point.

There's a reasonable interpretation of the book that humanity is nearly extinct and everyone in the novel is synthetic, except for Isidore. This is symbolized by the toad Deckard finds at the end of the book and thinks is a real animal, thought to be extinct, but is discovered to be synthetic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Then shouldn't the researcher know that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, but do androids dream of electric sheep?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ravens dream. This isn't remotely disputable. All birds and almost all mammals dream.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found the whole bold letters thing to be such a cheesy way to inject something into an otherwise great article, same with the "can ravens even dream" part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It probably ought to be local time, but it also probably should be based on corvid circadian timing (and therefore sunlight).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The anomalous properties of SCP-2106-1 become apparent between the hours of 2200 and 0800.1

Nitpick: I feel that the timezone should be addressed instead, rather than simply translating from 24 hour clock to a 12 hour system.

Other than that, I have little issues with this SCP.

Footnotes
1. 10:00 PM – 8:00 AM

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much improved from the version you showed me in chat. Easy upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love everything about this one!
